Color Theory.aiRubric: Assess understanding of color theory concepts.

lessons.title: Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary Colors
lessons.theory:
## The Color Wheel
Color theory starts with the color wheel. Primary colors are Red, Blue, and Yellow. Secondary colors are created by mixing primary colors (e.g., Green, Orange, Purple). Tertiary colors are created by mixing a primary and a secondary color.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Knowledge Check
What do you get when you mix blue and yellow?

lessons.title: Serif vs Sans-Serif
lessons.theory:
## Font Families
Serif fonts have small decorative lines (serifs) at the ends of characters, like Times New Roman. Sans-serif fonts do not have these lines, like Arial or Helvetica. Sans-serif is generally preferred for modern digital interfaces due to its clean look.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Classification
Is 'Helvetica' a serif or sans-serif font?
lessons.hint: It lacks the small decorative lines.

lessons.title: Low Fidelity vs High Fidelity
lessons.theory:
## Wireframing
A wireframe is a visual guide that represents the skeletal framework of a website. Low-fidelity wireframes are quick, rough sketches (often just boxes and lines). High-fidelity wireframes include actual content, specific spacing, and typography.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Identify Fidelity
If you are drawing boxes on a napkin to plan a layout, are you creating a low-fidelity or high-fidelity wireframe?

lessons.title: Qualitative vs Quantitative
lessons.theory:
## Types of Research
User research is how we understand user needs. Qualitative research involves non-numerical data like interviews and observations to understand *why* users do things. Quantitative research involves numerical data like surveys and analytics to understand *what* users do.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Method Selection
If you want to know exactly how many users clicked a specific button, should you use qualitative or quantitative research?

lessons.title: Interactive Mockups
lessons.theory:
## Prototyping
A prototype is an interactive mockup of your product. It allows you to test the user flow and interactions before writing any code. Tools like Figma and InVision are commonly used for this.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Purpose of Prototyping
True or False: Prototypes are fully functional applications with a working database.

lessons.title: WCAG and Contrast
lessons.theory:
## Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG)
Accessibility ensures your product can be used by people with disabilities. A critical aspect is color contrast. Text must have a high enough contrast ratio against its background to be readable by users with visual impairments.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Alt Text
What HTML attribute is used to provide alternative text for an image, essential for screen readers?

lessons.title: Consistency at Scale
lessons.theory:
## What is a Design System?
A design system is a collection of reusable components, guided by clear standards, that can be assembled together to build any number of applications. It ensures brand consistency and speeds up development.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Benefit Check
Does a design system increase or decrease development speed over time?
lessons.hint: You don't have to build things from scratch.

lessons.title: Data-Driven Decisions
lessons.theory:
## A/B Testing
A/B testing (or split testing) is the process of comparing two versions of a webpage or app against each other to determine which one performs better. Version A is the 'control' and Version B is the 'variant'.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Identifying the Control
In an A/B test, what do we call the original, unchanged version of the page?

lessons.title: Micro-interactions
lessons.theory:
## Enhancing User Experience
Interaction design focuses on creating engaging interfaces with logical and thought-out behaviors. Micro-interactions are small, single-purpose events found on devices and in apps (e.g., a 'like' button turning red when clicked).
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Example Recognition
Is a complex checkout flow considered a micro-interaction?

lessons.title: Sprints and Standups
lessons.theory:
## Agile Methodology
Agile is an iterative approach to project management. Scrum is a specific Agile framework. Work is broken down into short, time-boxed phases called 'Sprints' (usually 1-4 weeks). The team meets daily in a 'Standup' to discuss progress.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Scrum Roles
Who is responsible for maximizing the value of the product and managing the Product Backlog in Scrum?

The Product Lifecycle.aiRubric: Assess understanding of the product lifecycle.

lessons.title: From Idea to Sunsetting
lessons.theory:
## Product Stages
The typical software product lifecycle consists of: Ideation, Definition, Prototyping, Development, Testing, Deployment, Maintenance, and eventually Sunsetting (retiring the product).
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Final Stage
What is the term for officially retiring a product and removing it from the market?
lessons.hint: It relates to the sun going down.

lessons.title: Product Requirements Documents
lessons.theory:
## What is a PRD?
A Product Requirements Document (PRD) defines the value and purpose of a product or feature. It outlines *what* needs to be built, *who* it is for, and *why* it matters, without necessarily dictating exactly *how* it should be technically implemented.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: PRD Focus
Does a PRD primarily focus on the 'What and Why' or the 'How' (technical implementation)?
lessons.hint: It defines the requirements, not the code.

lessons.title: Launching Successfully
lessons.theory:
## GTM Basics
A Go-To-Market (GTM) strategy is an action plan that specifies how a company will reach its target customers and achieve a competitive advantage. It includes pricing, sales, marketing, and distribution channels.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: GTM Components
Which of the following is typically a key component of a GTM strategy: Code Architecture or Pricing Strategy?
lessons.hint: It's related to the market and sales.

lessons.title: Seed to Series A
lessons.theory:
## Startup Funding Rounds
Startups often raise money in rounds. 'Seed' is the initial capital used to start the business. 'Series A' usually follows when the company has a product and some traction, looking to optimize its user base and product offerings.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: First Round
What is the very first official funding round for a startup typically called?

lessons.title: Product-Market Fit
lessons.theory:
## Finding the Match
Before scaling aggressively, a startup must find Product-Market Fit (PMF). PMF means being in a good market with a product that can satisfy that market. Scaling before achieving PMF often leads to failure.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Prerequisite for Scaling
What is the acronym for the state a startup must achieve before trying to scale rapidly?

lessons.title: KPIs and Metrics
lessons.theory:
## Measuring Success
Product Analytics involves tracking how users engage with your software. Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) are the most important metrics you track (e.g., Daily Active Users - DAU, Churn Rate).
lessons.instructions:
## Task: User Retention
If a high percentage of users stop using your product after the first week, do you have a high or low 'Churn Rate'?
lessons.hint: Churn is the rate of users leaving.
